title: Finding the opposite side given the hypotenuse
problem: A right-angled triangle has a hypotenuse of $13$ cm and an angle of $40°$. Find the length of the side opposite the $40°$ angle, correct to 2 decimal places.

steps.instruction: Identify the known information and the unknown side.
Choose the ratio that links the opposite side and the hypotenuse. SOH: $\sin \theta = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$.
Evaluate using a calculator and round to 2 decimal places.

title: Finding the hypotenuse given the adjacent side
problem: In a right-angled triangle, the side adjacent to a $52°$ angle is $7$ m. Find the hypotenuse, correct to 2 decimal places.

steps.instruction: Identify the known information and the unknown.
Choose the ratio that links the adjacent side and the hypotenuse. CAH: $\cos \theta = \dfrac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$.
Rearrange to make $h$ the subject. Multiply both sides by $h$, then divide both sides by $\cos 52°$.
Evaluate using a calculator and round to 2 decimal places.

title: Finding a side in a real-world context
problem: A cable car wire runs from the base of a mountain to the summit. The wire makes an angle of $32°$ with the horizontal and the horizontal distance from base to summit is $850$ m. Find the length of the cable wire, correct to 2 decimal places.

steps.instruction: Draw and label a right-angled triangle. The horizontal distance ($850$ m) is the adjacent side, the wire is the hypotenuse, and the angle with the horizontal is $32°$.
Choose the ratio that links the adjacent side and the hypotenuse. CAH: $\cos \theta = \dfrac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$.
Evaluate using a calculator and round to 2 decimal places.
